description.summary: A giant humanoid cryptid reported from the forests of Sumatra, Indonesia, described as standing 7.5–12 feet tall, covered in dark bushy hair, and leaving very large footprints; sometimes called the Sumatran Bigfoot.
description.full: The Orang Gadang ('large person' in Malay/Indonesian) is a giant humanoid cryptid reported from the forests of Sumatra, particularly the Rokan area of Riau province. Local reports describe a bipedal creature 7.5–12 feet (approximately 2.3–3.7 meters) tall, covered in dark hair. In contrast to the much smaller orang-pendek (a separate Sumatran cryptid standing under 1.5 meters), the Orang Gadang is a genuinely giant figure. Footprints attributed to the creature have been found in the Rokan area, described as approximately 2 feet (61 cm) long and 8 inches (20 cm) wide with an unusually large big toe. Behavioral reports describe the creature as shy, nocturnal, and omnivorous — exploring campsites after dark, leaving behind large humanoid footprints, emitting unusual whistling sounds, and hurling stones from concealed positions. A possible historical reference appears in the 1375 Catalan Atlas of Abraham Cresques, which described burly men in the mountains of Sumatra twelve cubits (approximately 5.5 meters / 18 feet) in height, like giants. No specimen, photograph, or verified physical evidence has been confirmed. The Orang Gadang is distinct from and much larger than the orang-pendek.

etymology.origin: From Malay/Indonesian orang (person, people) and gadang (large, big). The name is a straightforward description: 'large person' or 'giant person.'
etymology.first_use: Modern cryptozoological literature; possible historical reference in the 1375 Catalan Atlas

related_phenomena.name: Orang Pendek
related_phenomena.description: Much smaller (under 1.5 m) Sumatran bipedal cryptid; distinct from Orang Gadang

related_phenomena.name: Bigfoot / Sasquatch
related_phenomena.description: North American large hairy bipedal cryptid; cross-cultural parallel

related_phenomena.name: Yeti
related_phenomena.description: Himalayan hairy hominid cryptid; cross-cultural parallel

related_phenomena.name: Orang Dalam / Orang Mawas
related_phenomena.description: Malaysian giant hominid cryptid; closely related tradition

features.description: Body covered in dark, bushy hair
Footprints reported approximately 61 cm (24 inches) long and 20 cm (8 inches) wide with an unusually large big toe
Substantially taller than a human; estimated 7.5–12 feet (2.3–3.7 m)

physical.morphology: A giant bipedal hominid with proportions broadly resembling a very large human or great ape. The reported foot size (61 cm) far exceeds human norms and is substantially larger than reported Bigfoot footprints in North America.

abilities.description: Hurls stones from concealed positions at observers or campsites
Explores campsites after dark, leaving behind large footprints

patterns: Throws stones when observed or disturbed

range.description: Reported from Sumatra, Indonesia, particularly the Rokan area of Riau province. May extend into other forested mountainous areas of Sumatra.

hotspots.name: Rokan area, Riau, Sumatra

location.description: Rokan river area, Riau province, Sumatra — location of reported large footprint finds

hotspots.notes: Large footprints (61 cm long, 20 cm wide) reportedly found here

first_recorded.source: Abraham Cresques, Catalan Atlas (1375) — possible reference to giant men in Sumatran mountains

origins.cultural_roots: The Orang Gadang tradition is embedded in local Sumatran folklore about giant forest-dwelling creatures. The Rokan area of Riau has produced the most specific footprint accounts.
origins.earliest_accounts: A possible reference appears in Abraham Cresques's 1375 Catalan Atlas, which described burly men in the mountains of Sumatra twelve cubits (approximately 5.5 m/18 ft) in height, like giants. Modern accounts are documented in cryptozoological literature.
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to a broader Southeast Asian tradition of giant forest beings, and to global Bigfoot-type traditions. Distinct from the smaller orang-pendek despite sharing Sumatra as a habitat.

timeline.event: Abraham Cresques's Catalan Atlas describes burly men in Sumatra's mountains twelve cubits tall, like giants
timeline.significance: Possible earliest historical reference to giant Sumatran humanoids

history.modern_era: The Orang Gadang is discussed in cryptozoological literature as a Southeast Asian parallel to Bigfoot. No systematic field investigation has been conducted. The creature remains entirely unverified by physical evidence.

physical.name: Rokan area footprints
physical.description: Large footprints reportedly found in the Rokan area of Riau, Sumatra — approximately 61 cm (24 inches) long and 20 cm (8 inches) wide with an unusually large big toe.
physical.location: Rokan area, Riau Province, Sumatra, Indonesia
physical.analysis: No independent scientific analysis documented. Size reported significantly larger than any known primate. Unverified.

scientific.name: Undescribed giant primate
scientific.description: The Orang Gadang represents an undescribed giant primate species surviving in Sumatra's remote forests — an extremely large hominid analogous to the proposed Gigantopithecus survival hypothesis applied in North American Bigfoot research.

skeptical.name: Misidentification and exaggeration
skeptical.description: Reports likely reflect misidentification of known large animals (sun bears, orangutans, large humans) in forest conditions where size assessment is difficult, combined with cultural exaggeration and the Bigfoot template introduced through media.
evidence_cited: Foot size exceeds biological plausibility for a primate
Known large primates (orangutans) exist in Sumatra and can be misidentified

traditions: Local Sumatran forest traditions of giant humanoid creatures
Possible historical memory encoded in the 1375 Catalan Atlas reference
folklore.beliefs: Local communities in the Rokan area of Riau reportedly have traditions of large forest beings leaving enormous footprints.
